attributes.description: Alerts when a load balancer receives zero routed requests over the evaluation window. Detects traffic drops that may indicate upstream DNS, routing, or client-side failures.
attributes.name: [AWS ELB OTel] Zero request traffic

investigation_guide.blob:
## Zero Request Traffic

A sudden drop to zero `RequestCount` on an ALB that normally serves traffic can indicate DNS failures, upstream routing changes, WAF blocks, or client connectivity issues — even when backends are healthy.

### Triage Steps
1. Confirm the ALB is expected to receive traffic during this window (exclude maintenance windows).
2. Check DNS resolution and Route 53 health checks for the ALB endpoint.
3. Verify listener rules, WAF associations, and security group ingress are unchanged.
4. Check whether `ELB 5XX` or `UnHealthyHostCount` explain the drop (backends down vs upstream failure).
5. Compare with CloudFront, API Gateway, or other upstream layers if applicable.

### Tuning
- Deploy only on ALBs that should always receive traffic; disable for dev/staging or intentionally idle load balancers.
- Adjust `timeWindowSize` — a longer window reduces false positives from brief quiet periods.

esqlQuery.esql:
FROM metrics-aws.elb.otel-*
| WHERE attributes.Namespace == "AWS/ApplicationELB"
    AND attributes.MetricName == "RequestCount"
    AND attributes.stat == "Sum"
| STATS requests = SUM(`metrics.amazonaws.com/AWS/ApplicationELB/RequestCount`)
    BY attributes.LoadBalancer, resource.attributes.cloud.region
// Zero requests in the window — deploy only on always-on production ALBs
| WHERE requests == 0
| SORT attributes.LoadBalancer ASC
